t: Deceptive behavior with no apparent material gain.
The behavior is not better explained by another mental disorder (delusional disorder, other psychotic).
Diagnosis is difficult — requires evidence (insurance documents, information from other clinics, video).
Clinical suspicion — atypical clinical presentations, multiple clinics, "impossible" symptom.
Obtain medical history from other clinics (with patient consent).
Observation — method of creating laboratory samples.
Clinical confirmation; however, factitious disorder may be comorbid (complex cases).
Conscious lie absent; patient feels symptoms as real.
Not conscious; functional disturbance of the nervous system.
("soft confrontation" — Eisendrath) — the diagnosis is explained to the patient within a framework of care rather than punishment; "your distress is real, we can help in another way".
Single physician model — all complaints are managed through that physician.
Limitation of invasive procedures and unnecessary examinations.
Confrontation abruptly 'burns' the patient; better in a gentle and caring context.
Eisendrath S.J. — soft confrontation and therapeutic alliance.
Explanation of diagnosis within a care framework instead of punishment.
— All complaints through the same physician.
Long-term therapeutic alliance and treatment of comorbidities improve prognosis.
Myth 1: "The patient is a 'liar', legal punishment is required"
Factitious disorder is a psychiatric disorder — response to emotional need; "soft confrontation" and psychotherapy are recommended.
Myth 2: "Directly saying 'you are lying' will 'correct' the patient"
a harsh confrontation causes the patient to "flee" and transfer to another clinic; a gentle approach builds a therapeutic alliance.
Factitious deliberate lying; somatic symptom — patient's genuine feeling.
Myth 4: "We must continue all invasive procedures"
Unnecessary invasive procedures are harmful and should be limited after a diagnosis is made.
Evidence base is limited, but improvement is possible with long-term therapeutic alliance and multidisciplinary care.
WHO. ICD-11. 6D50 Factitious disorder imposed on self. 2024.
Eisendrath S.J. Factitious physical disorders: treatment with the soft confrontation. Psychosomatics 1989;30(4):383–387.
Levenson J.L. (ed.). APA Publishing Textbook of Psychosomatic Medicine and Consultation-Liaison Psychiatry. 3rd ed. 2018.
